title: Rush footstool top
subtitle: A plaited rush seat for a simple wooden footstool frame
excerpt: A rush seat woven directly onto a simple wooden footstool frame. English rush (Juncus effusus), soaked and twisted into a thick strand, is wrapped and interlaced across the frame rails. The traditional domestic rush-work technique applied to a small square frame.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III for rush-seating technique. Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861) for the rush-work domestic tradition. Museum of English Rural Life open-access archive on rush-seating in British domestic chair-making.

glossaryTerms.definition: In rush work, the over-and-under weave used to fill the seat area after the corner triangles are established.
In rush work, a strengthening weave at the rail perimeter.
A pointed steel rod for opening a path through the weave to push in new material.

content.text: A rush footstool top is woven directly onto the four rails of a simple square wooden frame. English rush (also called bulrush or common rush) is the traditional material in Britain. The rush is soaked until pliable, then twisted into a continuous strand as it is worked onto the frame. The method builds up corner triangles first, then fills the square centre with a woven pattern.
Soak the rush in the trough for one to two hours before working. The rush should be pliable but not dripping. Work the rush while damp; it tightens as it dries and produces a firm seat.

Use a simple footstool frame with four corner posts and four rails. Rails should be 30 cm long on a small footstool and 45 cm on a larger one. The frame should be robust enough to take your foot weight. A frame made from 30 mm x 30 mm seasoned oak or ash sections is sufficient.

Tie the first rush strand to one corner post. Bring it across to the adjacent rail, wrap under the rail and back, then across to the next corner. Each sequence of four wraps (one at each corner) fills a small triangle at each corner of the frame.

to push each new row of rush under the previous rows at the corner triangles, keeping the rows tight. Continue the corner-building until the corner triangles meet at the centre of the rail on each side.

Once the corner triangles have closed the side rails, the centre gap is a square (or rectangle on a non-square frame). Fill it with a figure-eight weave: pass the rush over the front rail, under the back rail, over the back rail again, under the front rail, and repeat. This fills the centre with a tight woven pad.

Rush strands run out before the seat is complete. Join a new strand by knotting the butt end of the new strand to the tip end of the old one, keeping the knot on the underside of the seat. The dried seat hides the knots; they do not affect the surface.

Tie off the final strand under the seat. Trim all loose ends flush with secateurs. Let the seat dry fully over two to three weeks. The seat will firm up considerably as it dries. When fully dry, apply a light coat of boiled linseed oil to the rush using a cloth; this extends the life of the rush.
Drop-in chair seat: the same technique on a removable frame panel that drops into a chair seat opening.
Sea-grass substitute: sea-grass (pre-twisted, sold on rolls) simplifies the joining and produces a very similar result. Easier for beginners.

rows.cause: Rush not soaked long enough, or working area has dried out during the session
rows.fix: Re-soak the rush for a further two hours. During a long session, keep a damp cloth over the working section of rush to slow drying.

rows.symptom: Seat surface is uneven, some rows loose and some tight
rows.fix: Pack each completed row firmly before moving to the next. Press the row toward the corner triangle with the bodkin after each pass.
